User.title: User
User.description: Represents an authorized user account within the SecureEntry application. It stores identity information for access control based on pre-approved email addresses, relying on an external system for password authentication.

id.description: Unique identifier for the User entity.

email.description: The authorized email address of the user. It serves as a unique identifier for login and access control and is verified against pre-approved accounts. (Relationship: User 1:1 ExternalAuthSystem)

fullName.description: The full name of the user, primarily for display purposes within the application interface.

isAuthorized.description: A flag indicating whether this user's account is currently active and authorized to access the SecureEntry application, reflecting its 'pre-approved' status.

createdAt.description: The timestamp when this user account was initially created in the system.

updatedAt.description: The timestamp when this user account's information was last updated.

definition.description: Stores individual user profiles. The document ID (`userId`) MUST match the Firebase Authentication UID. This document includes the `isAuthorized` flag, which is crucial for determining a user's access to the SecureEntry application, directly supporting authorization independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user, corresponding to their Firebase Authentication UID.

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure for the SecureEntry application centers around a single top-level collection: `/users`. Each document within this collection will represent a user's profile, with the document ID corresponding directly to the Firebase Authentication UID (`request.auth.uid`).

**Authorization Independence:** This design achieves Authorization Independence by having each user's authorization status (`isAuthorized` field) directly embedded within their own user document. This eliminates any need for security rules to perform `get()` operations on parent documents or other collections to determine access rights. A user's access to their own profile, and by extension, the application, can be determined solely by checking `request.auth.uid == userId` and `resource.data.isAuthorized == true` within the rules applied to the `/users/{userId}` path. This enables atomic write operations without complex dependencies.

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters):** This structure inherently supports Query-Ability and Predictability (QAPs) by design:
1.  **Individual Access:** For read/write operations on a single user document (`/users/{userId}`), rules can simply enforce `request.auth.uid == userId` to ensure users can only access their own profile. The `isAuthorized` field acts as an additional direct check, preventing unauthorized but authenticated users from accessing the application's features.
2.  **List Operations:** For `list` operations, the current requirements do not necessitate regular users listing other user profiles. If an administrative feature were to be introduced later requiring a list of all authorized users, an administrator could perform a query on the `/users` collection filtered by `isAuthorized == true`. Security rules for such an operation would check for an explicit admin role (e.g., `exists(/roles_admin/$(request.auth.uid))`), ensuring the rule itself doesn't act as a filter but rather validates the caller's permission before the query executes. The structural segregation means all documents in `/users` share the same security posture (personal profiles), simplifying rules significantly.

This straightforward design aligns perfectly with the core principles, ensuring clarity, debuggability, and scalability for SecureEntry's authentication-focused features.
